title: Latent Convergence Modulation in Large Language Models: A Novel Approach to Iterative Contextual Realignment
authors: Patricia Porretta &Sylvester Pakenham &Huxley Ainsworth &Gregory Chatten &Godfrey Allerton &Simon Hollingsworth &Vance Periwinkle
abstract: Token prediction stability remains a challenge in autoregressive generative models, where minor variations in early inference steps often lead to significant semantic drift over extended sequences. A structured modulation mechanism was introduced to regulate hidden state transitions, ensuring that latent representation trajectories remain aligned with prior contextual dependencies while preserving generative flexibility. The modulation framework was designed to function within transformer-based architectures, dynamically constraining representation evolution without imposing external memory dependencies or extensive architectural modifications. Empirical evaluations demonstrated that structured latent adjustments contributed to reductions in perplexity fluctuations, entropy variance, and lexical instability, improving coherence in long-form text generation. Gradient propagation stability was further analyzed, revealing that the modulation process led to smoother optimization pathways, mitigating erratic fluctuations in weight updates across successive inference steps. The computational efficiency of the modulation process was assessed, showing that its integration within transformer-based architectures introduced only marginal overhead while maintaining compatibility with existing optimization frameworks. The structured modulation constraints also influenced syntactic variation, preventing excessive repetition while maintaining balanced sentence length distributions. Comparative evaluations against baseline models reinforced the role of controlled latent state evolution in improving pronoun resolution, logical consistency, and contextual alignment across autoregressive text generation tasks.

paragraphs: Natural language processing has seen rapid advancements in recent years, driven largely through the development and deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs). These architectures, built on deep neural networks with billions of parameters, have significantly improved machine-generated text quality, reasoning capabilities, and context-aware responses. Despite these improvements, limitations persist in maintaining coherence and consistency when generating extended sequences, particularly during iterative inference. As tokens are generated sequentially, minor deviations in early stages can compound over time, leading to semantic drift, logical inconsistencies, and a loss of contextual fidelity. Such issues arise due to the stochastic nature of token sampling, internal representation shifts, and accumulated divergence between intended and produced outputs. While existing techniques attempt to mitigate these problems via reinforcement learning, prompt engineering, or auxiliary memory mechanisms, they do not address the fundamental issue of convergence stability at the latent representation level.

To introduce a new perspective on stabilizing iterative inference, this study proposes Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM), a novel method designed to regulate the internal transformation of latent states within an LLM during text generation. Instead of relying solely on output-driven refinements, LCM operates within the hidden layers, ensuring that internal representations progressively align toward a stable trajectory across multiple decoding steps. Through this approach, the model maintains a more consistent mapping between early-stage activations and subsequent token predictions, reducing unintended semantic deviations. The modulation mechanism functions by introducing a structured constraint that adaptively guides hidden state transitions without compromising generative flexibility. By stabilizing internal activations, LCM seeks to improve coherence in longer outputs and minimize undesired variations that arise from iterative generation processes.

A key motivation for developing LCM is the observation that traditional LLM inference methods primarily focus on refining external outputs rather than directly addressing latent state dynamics. While fine-tuning methods and external reward-based optimizations attempt to adjust high-level behaviors, they often struggle to enforce consistency at the core representation level. As an alternative, LCM operates at the granularity of hidden activations, modulating their trajectories to prevent divergence while preserving the flexibility needed for natural text generation. This internal modulation approach introduces a principled mechanism that is both model-agnostic and adaptable to various transformer architectures without requiring extensive retraining or dataset modifications.

The objectives of this research are threefold. First, the study aims to systematically investigate latent representation divergence during iterative inference, identifying key factors contributing to instability. Second, an implementation of LCM is integrated into a recent open-source LLM, allowing for controlled evaluation of its effects on text coherence, generation stability, and representation alignment. Third, empirical analysis is conducted to assess the impact of LCM through quantitative metrics, measuring changes in perplexity drift, entropy variation, and inter-token consistency. The findings aim to offer new insights into improving LLMs through structured modulation of latent representations rather than external post-processing techniques.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. The next section reviews related works, providing an overview of existing methodologies addressing inference stability and contextual consistency in LLMs. Subsequently, the core theoretical framework behind LCM is introduced, followed by a detailed explanation of the implementation strategy and experimental setup. The results section presents empirical findings from controlled tests, illustrating the effectiveness of LCM in improving iterative inference stability. Finally, the discussion highlights broader implications, limitations, and potential future directions for enhancing LLM inference mechanisms through structured latent modulation.

The refinement of inference methodologies in Large Language Models (LLMs) has been a central focus in recent research, with a variety of strategies explored to enhance efficiency, accuracy, and coherence in generated outputs. Methods such as model pruning and quantization have been developed to reduce the computational demands of inference, ensuring that large-scale architectures can operate efficiently in environments with limited processing resources [1]. Knowledge distillation has been employed to transfer knowledge from larger models to smaller ones, enabling improved inference speed while preserving overall performance [2]. Selective context utilization techniques have been introduced to filter and prioritize relevant information from extensive input sequences, allowing models to maintain essential contextual elements without introducing excessive memory constraints [3]. Despite these advancements, existing inference methodologies often struggle with maintaining stability in long-form text generation, as minor deviations in early-stage token predictions can propagate through subsequent iterations, leading to substantial semantic drift [4]. While reinforcement learning-based approaches have been applied to refine inference pathways, they frequently require extensive computational resources and large-scale fine-tuning, making them impractical for widespread adoption in real-world applications [5].

In the area of context maintenance, research has explored mechanisms to preserve semantic consistency and mitigate loss of relevant information across extended sequences. Memory-augmented architectures have been introduced to incorporate long-term dependencies into the inference process, allowing models to retrieve relevant contextual cues from previous interactions rather than relying solely on immediate input tokens [6]. Hierarchical attention mechanisms have been implemented to dynamically adjust the weight of different input tokens based on their relative importance, preventing models from over-prioritizing recent tokens at the expense of distant but critical contextual elements [7, 8]. Segment-wise recurrent architectures have been designed to provide structured memory retention, ensuring that generated text remains semantically aligned with prior context without requiring excessive computational overhead [9]. Additionally, external retrieval-augmented approaches have been leveraged to supplement LLMs with knowledge from external databases, improving consistency in fact-based text generation tasks [10]. Despite these efforts, context maintenance remains a persistent challenge in LLMs, as existing techniques often introduce additional latency or require complex architectural modifications, which may hinder practical deployment [11].

Research into convergence mechanisms for LLM inference has sought to ensure that iterative generative processes yield stable and coherent outputs. The implementation of gradient clipping and adaptive learning rate schedules has been explored to prevent instability during training and inference, mitigating the risk of divergence in model outputs when processing long-form sequences [12, 13]. Regularization techniques, including dropout, weight decay, and batch normalization, have been applied to improve generalization by reducing the likelihood of overfitting and smoothing optimization trajectories [14]. Latent variable modeling has been used to introduce structured constraints on hidden state evolution, promoting more stable transformations of internal representations throughout inference steps [15]. Sequence-level likelihood maximization has been proposed as an alternative to traditional token-level optimization, encouraging models to generate text sequences that align more closely with global coherence objectives [16]. Furthermore, iterative refinement strategies, such as decoding re-ranking and constrained beam search, have been investigated to enforce consistency in model-generated outputs [17, 18]. However, these techniques often rely on post-hoc corrections rather than addressing instability at its root, leading to computational inefficiencies and limited applicability in real-time inference scenarios [19].

The proposed Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM) approach introduces a fundamentally different mechanism for addressing inference stability in LLMs, focusing directly on modulating internal latent representations during text generation. Instead of relying on external regularization or auxiliary memory structures, LCM operates at the level of hidden activations, guiding internal states toward stable convergence trajectories through structured modulation of latent transformations [20]. This methodology ensures that token-level predictions remain consistent with earlier latent activations, minimizing the compounding effects of error propagation in iterative inference [21]. Unlike reinforcement learning-based methods, which require extensive fine-tuning on large-scale datasets, LCM integrates seamlessly into existing architectures without introducing significant training overhead or requiring post-processing adjustments [22]. Furthermore, the modulation mechanism is designed to be adaptable across different transformer-based models, allowing for broad applicability without extensive hyperparameter tuning [23]. Through structured constraints on internal activations, LCM enhances coherence in generative tasks while maintaining the flexibility required for open-ended text generation [24]. This approach represents a significant departure from traditional inference refinement strategies, offering a novel pathway for improving long-form text consistency in LLMs without imposing prohibitive computational costs [25].

Despite substantial progress in optimizing LLM inference methodologies, challenges remain in achieving stable and reliable text generation over extended sequences. Existing approaches have made significant strides in improving efficiency, refining context utilization, and mitigating convergence instability, but their reliance on external modifications, auxiliary memory systems, or complex fine-tuning processes limits their practical deployment [26, 27]. The Latent Convergence Modulation approach addresses these gaps through a direct modulation of internal state transitions, offering a streamlined yet effective solution for improving inference stability in LLMs [28]. By aligning latent representations with structured constraints, this method provides a novel framework for enhancing the reliability and coherence of model-generated outputs, bridging critical gaps left unaddressed by prior research efforts [29].

The development of inference stabilization mechanisms in Large Language Models (LLMs) has remained a critical area of research, particularly in ensuring that iterative generative processes maintain contextual alignment across extended sequences. Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM) introduces an approach that directly influences the internal hidden state transitions of a model, refining the process through structured adjustments to latent representations. Unlike external post-processing strategies that rely on output corrections or auxiliary memory mechanisms, LCM functions as an intrinsic modulation layer within the model’s computational framework, reducing deviations that lead to semantic drift. Through adaptive constraints applied at the hidden representation level, LCM enables controlled latent transformations, improving the coherence and stability of generated text without imposing additional memory dependencies or computationally expensive re-ranking strategies. The methodology is designed to be model-agnostic, allowing integration into various transformer-based architectures while maintaining compatibility with conventional training paradigms.

subsections.title: 3.1 Conceptual Overview
paragraphs: Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM) was developed to mitigate inference instability through the direct modulation of hidden state transitions in a transformer-based LLM. The approach introduces a structured latent guidance function that constrains internal representations, preventing erratic deviations that typically arise during iterative generation. Unlike external memory-based context alignment techniques, which introduce additional retrieval steps, LCM operates within the model’s native representation space, ensuring that adjustments occur without requiring explicit memory augmentation. Through adaptive constraints on token representations, the modulation mechanism selectively refines activations in a manner that enhances contextual consistency while preserving model flexibility.

The theoretical foundation of LCM is grounded in structured modulation principles, where latent state convergence is guided through a controlled optimization framework. Rather than relying on fixed attention weight adjustments, the modulation mechanism dynamically adapts its constraints based on prior token dependencies, ensuring that successive states remain aligned with previously established trajectories. The modulation layer is designed to minimize long-term divergence, counteracting the compounding effects of generation drift that often lead to incoherent sequences. Through strategically altering the internal representation pathways, LCM enhances inference reliability while maintaining the generative adaptability required for diverse language modeling tasks.

subsections.title: 3.2 Mathematical Formulation
paragraphs: The latent state modulation mechanism was formalized through a structured optimization process that governs the evolution of hidden representations across iterative inference steps. Given an input sequence X={x1,x2,…,xn}X=\{x_{1},x_{2},\dots,x_{n}\}, a transformer-based model encodes it into a sequence of hidden states H={h1,h2,…,hn}H=\{h_{1},h_{2},\dots,h_{n}\}, where each hth_{t} represents the latent embedding at timestep tt. subsections.title: 3.3 Implementation Strategy
paragraphs: The integration of Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM) into a transformer-based Large Language Model (LLM) architecture was conducted through a structured modification of the hidden state propagation mechanism, ensuring that latent state adjustments occurred intrinsically within the self-attention layers. Rather than externally modifying attention weight distributions, the modulation function was embedded within the feedforward pathway of each transformer block, enabling dynamic adaptation of latent representations without requiring structural alterations to the existing model architecture. The modulation parameters were co-optimized with standard model weights, facilitating an adaptive convergence mechanism that responded dynamically to variations in input complexity and generative uncertainty.

Latent state transformations were constrained via an auxiliary loss function designed to maintain stable representation trajectories while preserving the model’s generative flexibility. The auxiliary objective was formulated as:

where ℒt​a​s​k\mathcal{L}_{task} represents the primary training objective (e.g., cross-entropy for language modeling), and α,β\alpha,\beta are weighting factors that balance modulation strength against generative performance. The modulation process was implemented as an auxiliary computational module within the transformer pipeline, ensuring compatibility with existing optimization frameworks while maintaining computational efficiency.

The structured modulation process followed a constrained optimization framework to regulate hidden state transitions while preserving contextual coherence. The implementation pipeline is summarized in Algorithm 1, detailing the sequential steps required for embedding LCM within a transformer-based inference mechanism.

The constrained optimization step ensured that latent representations remained within a controlled modulation regime, preventing excessive divergence while preserving adaptive flexibility. Through the integration of modulation constraints within the transformer block computations, the approach facilitated stable hidden state transitions without requiring additional memory augmentation or external inference refinements. The implementation framework provided a structured methodology for embedding LCM into generative inference pipelines, yielding improved consistency in token prediction trajectories while maintaining computational efficiency.

To evaluate the effectiveness of Latent Convergence Modulation in improving inference stability and contextual coherence, controlled experiments were conducted using a recent open-source LLM. The experiments were designed to measure the impact of LCM on output consistency, convergence properties, and overall text coherence across diverse generative tasks.

subsections.title: 4.1 Model and Dataset
paragraphs: The experimental framework utilized a state-of-the-art open-source LLM with a transformer-based architecture, incorporating pre-trained weights to facilitate transfer learning across multiple natural language processing tasks. The model was selected based on its architectural compatibility with LCM integration, ensuring that modulation constraints could be applied without requiring extensive reconfiguration.

For evaluation, a benchmark dataset containing long-form textual sequences spanning multiple domains was used to assess the model’s ability to maintain contextual coherence under different inference conditions. The dataset was structured to include controlled variations in sequence length, complexity, and topic shifts, allowing for a comprehensive analysis of modulation impact on generative stability. Preprocessing involved tokenization, normalization, and sequence partitioning to ensure consistent input representations across experimental conditions.

subsections.title: 4.2 Training and Evaluation Pipeline
paragraphs: The training process involved fine-tuning the pre-trained LLM with LCM-enabled modulation, optimizing both task-specific and modulation-specific loss functions to ensure stable latent state transitions. The optimization routine employed an adaptive learning rate schedule, allowing the modulation parameters to adjust dynamically based on input complexity and generative demands. Hyperparameters such as modulation weight λ\lambda, convergence threshold ϵ\epsilon, and auxiliary loss coefficients α,β\alpha,\beta were tuned to achieve an optimal balance between inference stability and generative flexibility.

Evaluation was conducted through a combination of quantitative and qualitative metrics, assessing the impact of LCM on inference consistency and text coherence. Perplexity reduction was used as a primary metric to quantify improvements in sequence-level predictability, while entropy variance measurements were employed to analyze stability in latent representations across inference steps. A controlled comparative analysis was performed against baseline models without modulation constraints, measuring divergence in token probability distributions and coherence preservation across extended generative sequences.

The experimental results were analyzed to determine the effectiveness of LCM in reducing semantic drift, improving long-term coherence, and enhancing inference stability. Additional ablation studies were conducted to isolate the contributions of different modulation components, identifying key factors influencing the effectiveness of latent convergence constraints in transformer-based LLMs.

The experimental evaluation of Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM) was conducted through a series of controlled trials designed to measure its impact on inference stability, contextual consistency, and overall generation quality in a transformer-based Large Language Model (LLM). The effectiveness of the modulation function was assessed through a combination of quantitative performance metrics, comparative evaluations against baseline models, and statistical analyses of token consistency over extended inference sequences. The results were structured into three key areas: convergence dynamics and stability across inference steps, perplexity variation and entropy distribution, and empirical assessments of contextual alignment with respect to long-form generative tasks.

subsections.title: 5.1 Convergence Stability and Hidden State Trajectories
paragraphs: The integration of LCM introduced structured constraints within the hidden state evolution process, influencing token-wise representation stability and ensuring controlled convergence trajectories. A comparative analysis of convergence rates was performed against a baseline transformer model without modulation constraints. The assessment involved measuring mean squared deviation between successive hidden state activations across inference steps, quantifying the extent of latent state stabilization achieved through LCM.

Table 1 presents a summary of the convergence stability metrics, showing the mean squared hidden state deviation across different layers of the transformer architecture. The results indicate a reduction in deviation magnitudes when LCM was applied, suggesting improved consistency in representation space transitions.
Figure 1 illustrates the evolution of latent state modulation over inference steps through a piecewise constant plot, demonstrating the extent of controlled variability across different layers.

subsections.title: 5.2 Perplexity Variation and Entropy Distribution
paragraphs: To assess the impact of LCM on generative stability, perplexity scores were computed across different test sets, measuring the average likelihood of predicted tokens under the model’s probability distribution. Lower perplexity values indicate improved predictive certainty, with more stable generative outputs across inference sequences. The entropy of token probability distributions was also analyzed, providing insights into uncertainty modulation during token sampling.

Table 2 summarizes perplexity values and entropy distributions for different input complexities. The application of LCM resulted in reduced entropy variation, highlighting its role in minimizing unexpected fluctuations in probability distributions.

subsections.title: 5.3 Empirical Contextual Alignment in Long-Form Generative Tasks
paragraphs: The empirical assessment of LCM’s effectiveness in improving contextual coherence was performed through an evaluation of token consistency across extended generative sequences. The analysis measured the retention of subject relevance, pronoun-antecedent agreement, and logical progression of ideas in generated text segments, comparing LCM-enhanced models with baseline versions.

Table 3 presents the percentage of generated sequences maintaining contextual alignment beyond a predefined coherence threshold. The LCM-enhanced model demonstrated improvements in all evaluated categories, reinforcing its role in ensuring more stable generative consistency.

The observed improvements in logical progression and pronoun resolution further reinforced the advantages of internal latent modulation in inference stabilization. The results collectively demonstrated the capability of LCM to enhance representation coherence, reduce instability in probability distributions, and improve overall generative alignment across varying levels of input complexity.

subsections.title: 5.4 Token Frequency Distributions and Lexical Stability
paragraphs: The consistency of token generation patterns was analyzed through frequency distributions across different experimental conditions, measuring the stability of lexical choices over extended sequences. The analysis focused on assessing whether the application of LCM influenced the model’s token selection tendencies, particularly in scenarios involving repeated exposure to similar prompts over multiple inference runs. Deviations in token frequency were measured using standard deviation across sampled sequences, with lower values indicating greater lexical stability.

Table 4 presents the standard deviation of token frequency for selected words across different inference settings. The application of LCM resulted in reduced variability in token occurrences, suggesting an improvement in lexical consistency without imposing excessive repetition constraints.

subsections.title: 5.5 Gradient Propagation Stability and Model Saturation
paragraphs: The impact of LCM on gradient propagation stability was assessed to determine whether modulation constraints influenced optimization behavior during backpropagation. The experiment measured the average magnitude of gradient updates across layers, quantifying the extent of stability improvements introduced through modulation constraints. Higher variability in gradient magnitudes could indicate instability in optimization trajectories, potentially leading to erratic convergence behavior.

Figure 2 presents a quiver plot visualizing the direction and magnitude of gradient updates across selected layers. The LCM-enhanced model exhibited more uniform gradient distributions, suggesting that modulation constraints contributed to smoother optimization pathways.

subsections.title: 5.6 Sentence Length and Syntactic Structure Complexity
paragraphs: To assess whether LCM had any unintended influence on syntactic structure or average sentence length, a statistical analysis was conducted on generated sequences across different experimental settings. The study measured the distribution of sentence lengths and analyzed complexity variations using parse tree depth as a structural indicator. A balanced distribution of sentence lengths without excessive uniformity was considered an indicator of stable generative behavior.

Table 5 presents the mean sentence length and parse tree depth across different categories of generated text. The LCM-enhanced model exhibited reduced fluctuations in syntactic complexity while maintaining a natural variation in sentence length distributions.

subsections.title: 5.7 Anomaly Detection in Semantic Consistency
paragraphs: To determine whether the application of LCM affected the occurrence of semantic inconsistencies, an anomaly detection framework was applied to identify segments where generated text deviated from expected meaning trajectories. The analysis involved automated clustering of semantic embeddings, detecting points where generated sequences exhibited abrupt contextual shifts. The proportion of detected anomalies was compared across baseline and LCM-enhanced models to evaluate the modulation’s impact on semantic coherence.

Figure 3 presents a histogram of detected anomalies per 1,000 generated tokens across different evaluation datasets. The results indicated a lower frequency of abrupt semantic shifts in the LCM-enhanced model, suggesting improved stability in generative coherence.

The experimental results provided insights into the role of Latent Convergence Modulation (LCM) in refining iterative inference stability and contextual consistency in Large Language Models (LLMs). The analysis revealed that latent state modulation introduced a structured mechanism for constraining hidden state deviations, reducing instability across extended generative sequences. The reduction in perplexity and entropy variance indicated that token prediction uncertainty was more controlled under LCM constraints, ensuring that probability distributions remained well-structured across inference steps. The empirical evaluation of contextual coherence demonstrated that LCM played a role in preserving logical consistency within generated text, mitigating the effects of semantic drift that typically arise from unconstrained autoregressive generation. The observed improvements in subject relevance, pronoun resolution, and logical sequence structuring suggested that latent modulation contributed to sustained coherence across different scales of text generation complexity.

The application of LCM within transformer-based architectures altered the underlying mechanisms of representation transition, influencing both forward propagation stability and optimization behavior. The observed reduction in gradient propagation variance suggested that modulation constraints smoothed optimization pathways, preventing excessive fluctuations in weight updates across successive training iterations. The structured latent adjustment framework allowed hidden representations to evolve along more controlled trajectories, minimizing erratic deviations in token-level probability distributions. The stabilization effect was particularly pronounced in long-form text generation tasks, where uncontrolled hidden state divergence often leads to inconsistency in semantic structuring and lexical selection. The empirical measurements of sentence length variation and syntactic complexity indicated that the modulation process maintained a balance between structural diversity and generative stability, avoiding excessive uniformity while ensuring that extended sequences adhered to coherent compositional frameworks.

The effectiveness of LCM in improving inference stability must be considered alongside potential trade-offs introduced through structured modulation constraints. While the introduction of controlled latent evolution improved token consistency and contextual alignment, the application of modulation functions imposed a supplementary computational burden during inference. The additional processing steps required for constrained hidden state adjustment introduced marginal latency, particularly when operating at higher modulation intensities. Although the computational overhead remained within acceptable ranges for practical deployment, the trade-off between generative flexibility and modulation-induced stability must be optimized based on specific application requirements. The constraints imposed on latent representations also introduced a potential risk of limiting the model’s ability to explore alternative generative pathways, particularly in highly open-ended text generation scenarios where maximal variability is required for creative or exploratory tasks.

Despite the observed advantages, further refinements to the modulation framework could improve adaptability across different inference conditions. The integration of adaptive modulation weighting, where the intensity of latent adjustment is dynamically tuned based on contextual complexity, may provide a mechanism for balancing stability against generative flexibility in a more nuanced manner. Future investigations into reinforcement-driven modulation strategies could enable an optimization paradigm where the model autonomously regulates modulation intensity based on evolving text coherence metrics. The findings collectively highlighted the potential of structured latent modulation as a viable approach for improving inference stability, with the possibility of further advancements through adaptive regulation mechanisms that account for contextual variability in generative tasks.

The study introduced Latent Convergence Modulation as a structured mechanism for regulating hidden state transitions in Large Language Models, establishing a methodological foundation for improving inference stability, contextual consistency, and generative reliability across extended sequences. The empirical evaluation demonstrated that latent modulation constraints contributed to reduced token probability divergence, enhanced coherence in long-form text generation, and mitigated the propagation of semantic drift, highlighting the advantages of internal representation regulation as an alternative to conventional external memory augmentation techniques. The observed reductions in entropy variation and perplexity fluctuations provided quantitative evidence of improved token selection stability, while the empirical assessment of pronoun resolution and subject retention reinforced the role of constrained hidden state adjustments in preserving linguistic continuity across autoregressive inference steps. The application of modulation functions within transformer architectures also influenced optimization behavior, yielding more stable gradient propagation patterns that resulted in controlled weight updates during training, preventing erratic fluctuations in token prediction pathways. The evaluation of syntactic complexity and sentence length variation further suggested that the modulation process introduced structured constraints without enforcing excessive uniformity, allowing for the preservation of diverse generative capabilities while reducing instability in lexical composition. The computational trade-offs associated with modulation constraints, although measurable, remained within practical bounds for real-world deployment, emphasizing that structured latent state control can be integrated into transformer-based architectures without imposing prohibitive overhead. The findings collectively substantiated the effectiveness of internal modulation mechanisms in improving inference coherence, providing an alternative pathway for enhancing generative consistency through direct regulation of representation evolution rather than reliance on external post-processing adjustments.
